coredata.dc:title: Nano hollow carbon regulated by carbon dots as a high-performance anode for potassium and sodium ion battery
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Yuli
Wang, Qing
Liu, Qing
Wang, Rongzhe
Wang, Yan
Luo, Shaohua
Zhang, Yahui
Hou, Pengqing
Yan, Shengxue
Liu, Xin
Guo, Jing
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, carbon dots (CDs) derived from citrus peel were used as regulators to adjust the size of template SiO2 and further prepare hollow carbon spheres with different diameter. During the synthesis process of SiO2 templates, the abundant surface functional groups of CDs interact strongly with the functional groups (hydroxyl groups, etc.) possessed by silicic acid and TEOS, thereby inhibiting the growth of SiO2 spheres. By changing the content of CDs added, the size of the SiO2 template changes accordingly. A series of nano-hollow carbon spheres were prepared through high-temperature treatment of as-obtained SiO2 spheres coated with PDA and then acid washing. Among them, HCN-60 with optimized size and hollow structure exhibited excellent potassium/sodium storage performance. This research provides a new idea for the rational design of high-performance carbon anodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sodium/Potassium-ion battery
Hollow structure
Anode

E2021501007 , E2021501029 , E2022501029 and E2022501028 ), the Natural Science Foundation-Steel, the Iron Foundation of Hebei Province (No. E2022501030 ), the Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department ( ZD2022158 ), the Central Guided Local Science and Technology Development Fund Project of Hebei Province ( 226Z4401G ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( N2023040 , N2123035 , N2223010 and N2223009 ), Performance subsidy fund for Key Laboratory of Dielectric and Electrolyte Functional Material Hebei Province (No. 22567627H ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) TEM image of CDs; (c)XRD pattern of CDs; (d) FTIR spectrum of CDs.
SEM images: (a) HCS; (b) HNC-30; (c) HNC-60; (d) HNC-100.
TEM images of (a, b) HCS;(d, e) HNC-30;(g, h) HNC-60;(j, k) HNC-100, respectively; HRTEM images of (c) HCS;(f) HNC-30;(i) HNC-60;(l) HNC-100, respectively.
(a) XRD pattern; (b) Raman spectra; (c) FTIR spectrum.
The CV curves of HNC-60 collected at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1.
The charge and discharge curves at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 (a) HCS; (b) HNC-30; (c) HNC-60; (d) HNC-100.

Electrochemical K storage performance of HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60 and HNC-100 (a)Rate performance; (b) Cycling performance at a current density of 0.1 A g−1;(c) Cycling performance at a current density of 0.5 A g−1;(d) Cycling performance at a current density of 1 A g−1.

Kinetic analysis of electrochemical K storage for HNC-60 (a)CV curves at various sweep speeds;(b) Logarithmic fit plot of peak current versus voltage at various sweep speeds;(c) the pseudocapacitance contribution at a sweep rate of 1 mV s−1;(d) Pseudocapacitance contribution at different scan speeds.

(a) GITT Voltage Curve of the HNC-60 and HCS anodes; (b) The exhaustive calculation procedure of the D-value; (c) DK + of the HNC-60 and HCS anodes during the discharge process. (d) DK + of the HNC-60 and HCS anodes during the charge process.
The CV curves of HNC-60 collected at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1.

Electrochemical Na storage performance of HCS and HNC-60 Charge-discharge curves of the first three cycles at a current density of 0.05 A g−1:(a) HCS; (b) HNC-60;(c) Rate performance; (d) Cycling performance at 0.1 A g−1;(e) Cycling performance at 1 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Nano hollow carbon regulated by carbon dots as a high-performance anode for potassium and sodium ion battery

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, carbon dots (CDs) derived from citrus peel were used as regulators to adjust the size of template SiO2 and further prepare hollow carbon spheres with different diameter. During the synthesis process of SiO2 templates, the abundant surface functional groups of CDs interact strongly with the functional groups (hydroxyl groups, etc.) possessed by silicic acid and TEOS, thereby inhibiting the growth of SiO2 spheres. By changing the content of CDs added, the size of the SiO2 template changes accordingly. A series of nano-hollow carbon spheres were prepared through high-temperature treatment of as-obtained SiO2 spheres coated with PDA and then acid washing. Among them, HCN-60 with optimized size and hollow structure exhibited excellent potassium/sodium storage performance. This research provides a new idea for the rational design of high-performance carbon anodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely used in our daily life and production. However, due to the limited resources of lithium and the increase in cost[1,2], the development of alternative rechargeable batteries with abundant resources has become a one of research highlights [3,4]. Considering the abundant reserves of potassium and sodium on earth[5], potassium-ion batteries (PIBs) and sodium-ion batteries (NIBs) are considered as potential LIBs alternatives and have been extensively studied[6].

Carbon materials are considered ideal energy storage materials due to their stable performance and abundant resources[7]. Graphite has become a widely used commercial anode for LIBs. However, compared with the smaller radius (0.076 nm) of Li+, the larger radius of Na+ (0.102 nm) and K+ (0.138 nm) and the limited interlayer space of the graphite usually lead to low reversible capacity, poor cycling stability, which seriously hinder the development of SIBs and PIBs[8]. Unlike graphite with long-range ordered structure, amorphous carbon is an ideal choice for preparing high performance SIBs and PIBs due to its large interlacing distance and high defect density[9,10]. Nano hollow carbon, as a new type of three-dimensional nanostructured carbon material, has the characteristics of high specific surface area, coexistence of different pore sizes, usable inner cavity, easy doping and adjustment[11,12]. The hollow structure of nano hollow carbon can not only facilitate large-scale transportation by shortening ion diffusion distance, but also prevent the destructive volumetric expansion of anode materials during charging/discharging cycles[13,14].

By adjusting the size of nano hollow carbon spheres, the ion diffusion distance can also be shortened, as a result, the material's electrochemical performance is enhanced[15]. The methods for preparing hollow carbon spheres include template method, liquid phase method, and gas phase method[16–19]. In all template methods, “silica-assisted” synthesis is a strategy for preparing size-controlled hollow carbon spheres employing hexadecyl trimethylammoniumchloride(CTAC) as template, phenolic resin as polymer precursor, and silicate oligomers as inorganic precursor[20]. Due to the influence of CTAC, the diameter of the prepared hollow carbon spheres is not less than 180 nm. It is inevitable to choose other surface regulators to prepare smaller carbon spheres.

Carbon dots(CDs) are zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm, low toxicity, high chemical stability, and abundant surface functional groups[21]. The controllable surface structure of CDs makes it easy to functionalize through covalent and non-covalent bonds. Therefore, CDs can be used as connectors and inducers to construct nanostructures with different morphologies.

In this paper, CDs were first prepared from orange-peel by hydrothermal method, and then used as size adjuster to prepare a size controllable core–shell structure hollow carbon. The nano-hollow carbon material is employed as the anode of SIBs and PIBs and exhibits exceptional electrochemical performance.

Orange peel was purchased from the supermarket, ammonia and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were bought from Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd., dopamine came from Shanghai Civic Chemical Technology Co., Ltd.. The chemicals used are all analytical grade and do not require further purification.

First, the orange peel was ground into powder, and added to the polytetrafluoroethylene liner with deionized water and heated continuously at 180℃ for 6 h. After as-obtained samples were centrifuged, the gained supernatant was performed suction filtration, and then the filtrate was placed into a dialysis bag for dialysis for 48 h. The CDs powder was finally obtained by freeze-drying in a freeze-dryer for 48 h.

Ammonia, ethanol and a certain amount of CDs powder (0, 30, 60, and 100 mg) were added to deionized water and stirred the mixture for 30 min. TEOS and a mixture were combined and stirred for 15 min to produce solution (a). Dopamine powder was added to deionized water to obtain solution (b). Then the mixture of solution (b) and solution (a) was stirred for 20 min to gain black precipitate. The black precipitate washed with ethanol and water was carbonized at 800℃ for 2 h. The obtained product was ground and washed with 20 % HF solution to obtain hollow carbon. The samples added with 0, 30, 60, and 100 mg were CDs powder designated as HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60, and HNC-100, respectively.

The microstructures were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20). The experiment was carried out using X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique with Cu Ka radiation and Raman spectroscopy(HORIBA LabRAM HR Evolution). The molecular structures were observed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR-8400 s). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to perform elemental analysis on the samples. Pore structures were measured by a surface area analyzer (Autosorb iQStation 1). Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) is used to measure the molecular weight and molecular weight distribution of CDs (Agilent PL-GPC50).

The entire assembly process of the CR2032 coin battery is conducted in a Ar-filled glove box. 80 wt% of the active material, along with 10 wt% of acetylene black and 10 wt% of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), are combined and applied as a coating onto a clean copper foil. The counter electrode in this experiment was made of metallic sodium or potassium, while the separator was made of glass fiber. Electrolytes were composed of 0.8 M KPF6（NaClO4）in ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC) (1/1 vol%). Galvanostatic charge–discharge and cycling performance tests were performed using Neware test system. Measurements of cyclic voltammetry(CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy(EIS) were conducted on CHI 1000 electrochemical workstation.

The microstructure of CDs was examined by TEM. From the images shown in Fig. 1 (a, b), it is apparent that the CDs produced from orange peel display a consistent size of approximately 10 nm and exhibit excellent dispersion. The crystal structure of CDs was measured by XRD. In Fig. 1c, CDs have an amorphous carbon phase. The surface molecular structure of CDs was analyzed by FTIR. From the FTIR spectrum in Fig. 1d, it can be found that the surface of CDs has abundant nitrogen and oxygen containing functional groups.

In Fig. S1a, we used XPS to analyze the surface components of the CDs. The XPS spectrum provides that CDs are composed of carbon and oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups. Fig. S1b displays the XPS spectrum of C1s in high resolution. The peaks observed at energy levels of 284.88 eV, 286.33 eV, and 287.8 eV correspond to the presence of C–C, C-OH, and C = O bonds, respectively. From the detailed spectrum of the N1s element shown in Fig. S1c, it can be determined that the peaks at 399.8 eV and 400.2 eV correspond to the N–H and C-N bonds, respectively. Fig. S1d displays the detailed spectrum of O1s, which exhibits distinct peaks at 530.2 eV, 531.6 eV, and 532.2 eV corresponding to C-O-C, C = O, and C-OH, respectively.

GPC was used to measure the molecular weight of CDs. Table S1 shows the statistical absolute molecular weight of CDs. In the table, Mn is the number-average molecular weight, and Mw represents the weight-average molecular weight. PD, a polydispersity coefficient, is equal to Mw divided by Mn and represents molecular weight distribution. The closer the PD is to 1, the smaller the molecular weight distribution and the more concentrated the molecular weight. From the table, CDs possess a Mw of 14537 g mol−1 and a Mn of 11983 g mol−1, the PD value of 1.213 reflects a narrow molecular weight distribution of CDs.

The as-obtained CDs were used as size adjuster to prepare core–shell structure hollow carbon. The microstructure of the samples was investigated by SEM. The scanning images of HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60 and HNC-100 are showed in Fig. 2 , respectively. It can be observed that the HCS consists of monodisperse carbon spheres with a size of approximately 300 nm in Fig. 2a. Some broken spheres indicate the hollow structure of the spheres. Fig. 2 b-d are SEM images of HNC-30, HNC-60, and HNC-100, respectively. In Fig. 2b, the hollow structure of HNC-30 is observed and its size is significantly smaller than that of HCS. Fig. 2c shows that HNC-60 exhibits more smaller size than HNC-30 and is characterized by a worm-like structure. When 100 mg of CDs were added, HNC-100 in Fig. 2d shows an agglomerated particle structure.

All samples were measured using TEM. Fig. 3 a, b are the TEM images of HCS, in which the hollow structure of carbon spheres and the size of HCS can be clearly observed. Fig. 3d, e and Fig. 3g, h are the TEM images of HNC-30 and HNC-60, respectively. From the images, it can be observed that HNC-30 and HNC-60 also show obvious hollow structure with size of about 90 nm and 50 nm, respectively, indicating that the addition of CDs promotes the reduction of the size of central carbon. In addition, the worm-like structure of HNC-60 in the SEM image is composed of spheroidal hollow carbon. The size change of the hollow carbon materials is mainly due to the influence of CDs on the formation of SiO2 spheres, which leads to the change of the micro-morphology of the hollow carbon materials.

In this experiment, SiO2 spheres were synthetized by sol–gel method using TEOS hydrolysis and polycondensation in alkaline environment. This process can be divided into three parts: (1) TEOS was hydrolyzed to generate hydroxyl oxides-monosilicic acid (Si(OH)4) and ethanol; (2) Dehydration condensation and dealcohol condensation reactions occur between the generated silicic acids or between silicic acid and TEOS. Si-O-Si begins to form, and small and dispersed colloidal particles appear in the reaction system; (3) The as-obtained oligomers further aggregate to form a three-dimensional extended long chain skeleton structure. When CDs are added, the abundant functional groups on their surface might generate strong interface interaction hydroxyl groups on silicic acid or groups on TEOS, thereby inhibiting the condensation reaction and making it difficult for SiO2 particles to grow.

Fig. 3j,k show the TEM images of HNC-100. Due to the excessive addition of CDs, SiO2 is almost unable to grow, resulting in HNC-100 still having a solid structure. Fig. 3 c, f, i and l show HRTEM images of the four samples, respectively. The HRTEM image indicates that all the samples exhibit comparable amorphous structures and disordered carbon phases, suggesting the presence of characteristics associated with hard carbon[22].

XRD and Raman spectroscopy were utilized to analyze the phase characteristics of the samples. In Fig. 4 a, all samples have two wide diffraction peaks at about 24° and 43° corresponding (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphite structure, respectively, which indicates typical peaks for amorphous carbon materials[23]. Based on the Bragg equation, the interlayer spacing (d002) is 0.37 nm, which greater than 0.34 nm of graphite-like materials. The larger interlayer spacing is helpful to improve the electrochemical kinetics. From Raman spectrum of the samples(Fig. 4b), two obvious peaks at about 1334 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1, namely D peak (amorphous) and G peak (graphitization) can be observed[24]. ID/IG reflects the ratio of degree of graphitization and the degree of structural disorder defects. The smaller the ID/IG value, the lower the degree of disorder, and the more regular and ordered the structure. The ID/IG values of HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60 and HNC-100 are 1.05, 1.09, 1.11 and 1.02, respectively, indicating their disorder carbon structures. The presence of nitrogen and oxygen containing functional groups on the surface of the prepared samples was confirmed by FTIR analysis in Fig. 4c. This indicates that the samples have a high abundance of these functional groups.

The XPS technique was used to analyze the surface chemical properties of the samples. In Fig. S2a, the XPS full spectrum shows that all samples contain C, N and O elements. The existence of N1s peak proves that the nitrogen element in polydopamine is retained during carbonization. Fig. S2c, f, i, l shows the C1s high resolution spectra of HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60 and HNC-100, respectively. The presence of three distinct peaks around 284.8 eV, 285.5 eV, and 289.0 eV can be attributed to the O = C–C, C-N, and C–C bonds, respectively. Fig. S2d, g, j and m are the N1 s high-resolution spectra of HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60 and HNC-100, respectively. After fitting, there are three peaks at 403.64 eV, 400.96 eV and 398.4 eV, and these peaks can be attributed to the presence of graphite N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N, respectively.

As shown in Fig. S2b, the contents of different kinds of nitrogen elements in the sample are shown. Compared with graphite nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen located at the edge of carbon have stronger chemical activity. Pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen will produce a large number of defects, which are easy to accept electrons and are conducive to rapid diffusion of ions. Fig. S2 e, h, k and n are the O1 s high-resolution spectra of HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60 and HNC-100, respectively. The three peaks observed at energy levels of 530.9 eV, 532.1 eV, and 533.3 eV correspond to the functional groups COOH, C-O-C, and C = O, respectively. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups enhances the ability of electrode materials to interact with water on their surface, making them more wettable. Additionally, these functional groups also help in attracting and binding sodium or potassium ions onto the electrode material.

The pore structures of all carbon were analyzed utilizing N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. In Fig. S3, the HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60, and HNC-100 have specific surface areas of 557.4 m2 g−1, 721.9 m2 g−1, 733.3 m2 g−1, and 115.4 m2 g−1, respectively. The isotherms of all samples have typical type IV isotherms, indicating that the samples have microporous/mesoporous hierarchical structure. The smaller specific surface area of HNC-100 is attributed to solid block structure. From the Fig. S3 b, d, f and h, it can be observed that HNC-30 and HNC-60 exhibit more prominent peaks in the microporous area compared to HCS. This can be attributed to the internal pyrolysis of CDs, resulting in the generation of a significant amount of micropores and mesopores. While HNC-100 is a solid structure, the pores in the microporous and mesoporous regions are less than HCS.

The samples were employed as the anode for PIBs and NIBs. Fig. 5 is the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of HNC-60 anode for PIBs. In the initial phase, there are two peaks observed at approximately 0.29 V and 0.48 V in the curve. These peaks suggest that the electrolyte undergoes decomposition and results in the formation of a solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film on the anode's surface. In the subsequent cycle process, the disappearing of two peaks indicates that SEI was stably formed in the first cycle and no longer reacted in the following cycle. A distinct peak is observed at approximately 0.01 V on the cathode, which reflects the intercalating of K+ into the carbon layer to form KC8 [25] . A broad anodic peak appears near 0.5 V, corresponding to the disembedding of K+ from the carbon anode. Furthermore, the following two CV curves exhibit a nearly identical shape, suggesting excellent reversibility of the anode.

Fig. 6 displays the galvanostatic charge and discharge profiles of potassium ion half-cells for various cycles (1st, 3rd, 5th, 50th, 100th, and 200th) at a current density of 0.1 A g−1. The HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60, and HNC-100 materials exhibit initial discharge and charge capacities of 1240.9, 1586.2, 274.6, 333.8 and 207.3 mAh g−1, 1305.5 mAh g−1, 177.7 mAh g−1, 729.7 mAh g−1, respectively, and their initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) are16.7 %, 17.3 %, 25.6 %, and 24.3 %, respectively. The reason for low ICE is due to the increased utilization of electrolyte to create a SEI film, resulting in a greater decrease in capacity. In the subsequent charge–discharge curves, the coulombic efficiency increased rapidly and gradually approached 100 %, indicating that the prepared samples had good cycle stability.

The rate capability of the samples was assessed by changing the current densities, as depicted in Fig. 7 a. The reversible capacities of HCS are 209.8, 176.8, 154.2, 136.9, 120.0 and 99.5 mAh g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 A g−1, respectively. With the reduction of the current density to 0.1 A g−1, the reversible capacity is restored to 183.0 mAh g−1. Similarly, at the same current density, the capacity of HNC-30 is 272.7, 216.4, 191.7, 171.2, 151.7, and 127.9 mAh g−1, while the capacity of HNC-60 is 331.2, 264.2, 230.9, 203.8, 177.9, and 144.6 mAh g−1 respectively, and the capacity of HNC-100 is 184.7, 158.4, 135.8, 117.3, 97.3 and 51.4 mAh g−1, respectively. As the current density regains to 0.1A g−1, the capacities of HNC-30, HNC-60 and HNC-100 are recovered to 225.1 mAh g−1, 272.5 mAh g−1 and 164.8 mAh g−1, respectively. The findings indicate that HNC-60 exhibits better rate capability when compared to the other samples.

Fig. 7b shows the cycling performance of HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60 and HNC-100 anodes at 0.1 A g−1 for 200 cycles. After 200 cycles, the capacities of HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60, and HNC-100 maintain at 189.5, 211.1, 252.3, and 150 mAh g−1, respectively. In Fig. 7c, when the current density is increased to 0.5 A g−1, the reversible capacities of HCS, HNC-30, HNC-60 and HNC-100 after 500 cycles remain at 223.4 mAh g−1, 129.9 mAh g−1, 152.0 mAh g−1 and 96.4 mAh g−1, respectively. The capacity of HNC-60 remains at 194.2 mAh g−1 even after 1000 cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1, as shown in Fig. 7d. The results further prove the exceptional cycle stability of HNC-60 anode.

To investigate the process by which charge is stored in the HNC-60 anode material, CV curves of HNC-60 anode are examined at scanning rates from 0.1 mV s−1 to 1 mV s−1 (Fig. 8 a). The energy storage process of anodes consists of two parts: surface pseudocapacitance behavior and diffusioncontrol behavior, which can be analyzed using equations (1) and (2):
(1)
i = a
v
b
(2)
l o g ( i ) = l o g ( a ) + b l o g ( v )
where i is current, v is sweeping rate, and a is a constant[21]. The value of b can be calculated by analyzing the plot created by log (i)-log (v). The value of b is approximately 0.5, suggesting that the process is primarily influenced by diffusion control. When the value of b is approximately 1, the surface capacitance behavior governs the storage process. As shown in Fig. 8b, log (i)-log (v) curves were plotted according to CV curves at different sweeping rates. The cathodic peak had a b value of 0.95, while the anodic peak had a b value of 0.96, meaning that the charge storage process of HNC-60 electrode was mainly controlled by capacitive process, which resulted in rapid intercalation/deintercalation of potassium ions and long cycle life.

Furthermore, the mixed mechanism contribution of diffusion and capacitance control can be measured by equation (3)
(3)
i ( V ) =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
#
where k 1 v is related to capacitance and k 2 v 1/2 is diffusion. The values of k1 and k2 can be determined by fitting the slope and intercept of the line by i (V)/v 1/2- v 1/2.

Fig. 8c shows the pseudocapacitance and diffusion contributions at 1 mV s−1, the purple area represents the contribution of pseudocapacitance. From the Fig. 8d, the contribution of capacitance at scan rates of 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7 and 1 mV s−1 are 65.9 %, 77.1 %, 84.7 %, 89.9 % and 94.3 %, respectively. By demonstrating the increase in scanning rate, we observe that there is an increase in the contribution of capacitance.

The charge transfer characteristics of the different electrodes were investigated using EIS measurements. In Fig. 9 , the Nyquist plots of all anodes shows two distinct regions. The charge transfer resistance is represented by a depressed semicircle when observed at high frequencies[25], while the ion diffusion resistance is indicated by an oblique line at low frequencies. Obviously, the charge transfer resistance and diffusion resistance of HNC-60 anode is lower than that of the other anodes, which can effectively promote the transport of charge and potassium ions in the anode.

In addition, the electrochemical kinetics of HNC-60 and HCS were studied by galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT), as shown in Fig. 10 a. It can be found that the HNC-60 anode shows a small voltage hysteresis. In addition, the corresponding diffusion coefficient (D) of HNC-60 and HCS during discharge and charging can also be calculated, as shown in Fig. 10b. According to Fig. 10c, d, the D value of HNC-60 is always higher than that of HCS, indicating its faster K+ diffusion kinetics.

SEM was used to observe the microstructures of the HNC-60 anode after cycling (Fig. S4). It can be seen that the microstructures of HNC-60 remained intact, and no apparent cracks were observed on the surface, indicating its good structural stability.

HCS and HNC-60 were further utilized as anodes for NIBs. The CV curves of HNC-60 at 0.01–3.0 V are showed in Fig. 11 . Two large irreversible cathodic peaks appear near 0.43 and 0.87 V and disappear in subsequent cycles, ascribing to the formation of SEI layer. The distinct cathodic peak observed at 0.01 V can be attributed to the process of Na+ ions being inserted into carbon-based materials. The broad anodic peak of 0.1 V ∼ 0.5 V reveals the sodium ion extraction from the HNC-60 anode. In addition, the following CV curve overlap well, which indicate that the anode has good cycle stability.

Fig. 12 a, b are the discharge and charge curves of HCS and HNC-60 anodes at 0.05 A g−1. The initial discharge and charge capacities of HCS and HNC-60 are 1486.7 mAh g−1 and 210.4 mAh g−1, 2252.3 mAh/gand 432.4 mAh g−1, respectively. and the ICE is 14.2 % and 19.2 %, respectively. The following charge–discharge curves almost coincide, revealing good cycling stability of the anode.

The rate property of HCS and HNC-60 anodes was further measured (Fig. 12c). At 0.1, 0.2, 0.5 1, 2 and 5A g−1 , the specific capacities of HNC-60 in the reversible state are 357.6, 250.3, 217.2, 186.1, 167.2 and 155.6 mAh g−1, respectively. When the current density decreases to 0.1 A g−1, the HNC-60 has a capacity of 289.6 mAh g−1. Compared with HCS, HNC-60 with smaller nano hollow structures exhibits superior rate performance.

The figure depicted in Fig. 12d illustrates the extended cycling performance of the HCS and HNC-60 anodes when operated at a current density of 0.1 A g−1. The reversible specific capacities of HCS and HNC-60 after 150 cycles are 170.4 mAh g−1 and 269.2 mAh g−1, respectively. Fig. 12e illustrates that the HNC-60 material has an initial charge capacity of 245.9 mAh g−1 when tested at a current density of 1A g−1. After 1000 cycles, the capacity remains constant at 227.1 mAh g−1, and the capacity retention rate is 92.4 %. The results show that HNC-60 material has excellent long-cycle performance.

In order to more specifically study kinetic characteristics of HNC-60 as a sodium storage anode, the CV curve of HNC-60 was tested at 0.1 mV s−1 ∼ 1 mV s−1 (Fig. S5a). Using the same method as above, the b values of the cathodic and anodic peaks are 0.86 and 0.99 (Fig. S5b), respectively, implying its surface capacitance dominant behavior. Furthermore, the surface capacitance contribution at 1 mV s−1 is showed in Fig. S5c, where the inner red region indicates the surface capacitance contribution. In Fig. S5d, the pseudocapacitance contribution increases from 66.5 % to 94.2 % from 0.1 to 1 mV s−1, implying that the high-rate performance of HNC-60 mainly derives from the high surface capacitance control process.

The charge transfer characteristics of HNC-60 anode were further investigated by EIS, as shown in Fig.S6. It is evident that the HNC-60 material exhibits considerably lower charge transfer resistance and Warburg impedance compared to HCS. This suggests that HNC-60 possesses favorable electron/ion charge transfer kinetics. Similarly, the corresponding D values of HNC-60 and HCS during discharge and charging were calculated by GITT, as shown in Fig. S7a, b. The D value of HNC-60 is still larger than that of HCS, which further proves that the regulation of CDs accelerates the Na+ diffusion kinetics of hollow carbon spheres.

Table S2 shows a performance comparison of HNC-60-based anodes for PIBs/ SIBs between our work and other literature reports [26–30]. Compared to other anodes, HNC-60 exhibits such an excellent electrochemical performance, and some possible reasons have to be explained. Firstly, the HNC-60 possesses a considerable specific surface area which allows for an increased number of active sites. thereby increasing its sodium/potassium storage capacity. Secondly, the optimized structures and size of hollow carbon spheres regulated by CDs are conducive to ion diffusion and improve reaction kinetics. Thirdly, the hollow carbon spheres structures can effectively buffer the volume expansion caused by sodium/potassium ion insertion, thereby improving cycling stability.

CDs were firstly prepared from orange peel by hydrothermal method, and then the as-obtained CDs were used as size adjuster for silica-assisted preparation of hollow carbon spheres. Utilizing the interaction between the rich surface functional groups of CDs and the silicate or TEOS surface groups during the formation of SiO2 template to regulate its formation size, a series of hollow carbon spheres with different pore sizes were prepared. The results show that HNC-60 anodes own the best electrochemical potassium/sodium storage performance. As the anode of PIBs, the capacity of HNC-60 is still 252.3 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.1 A g−1. After 1000 cycles, the reversible capacity of the material remains at 194.2 mAh g−1 even at a current density of 1 A g−1. The HNC-60 material shows a reversible capacity of 269.2 mAh g−1 after 150 cycles at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 when used as the anode material in SIBs. Even at a current density of 1 A g−1, the reversible capacity of the material remains stable at 227.1 mAh g−1 even after undergoing 1000 cycles. The outstanding performance of HNC-60 originates from its reasonable size and hollow structure, shortening the ion diffusion distance and alleviating the volumetric expansion during charging and discharging.

Yuli Wei: Writing – original draft. Qing Wang: Writing – review & editing. Qing Liu: Data curation. Rongzhe Wang: Data curation. Yan Wang: Data curation. Shaohua Luo: . Yahui Zhang: . Pengqing Hou: . Shengxue Yan: Data curation. Xin Liu: . Jing Guo: .
